biography.title: Title
biography.text: John Lewis is a writer, educator, and cultural chronicler based in Houston, Texas, he brought with him a Southern sensibility and a deep curiosity about people — what drives them, what they create, and how they connect

biography.title: Title
biography.text: A passionate educator, John has spent years nurturing the minds of others while quietly building a body of written work that reflects his wide-ranging interests and sharp observational voice. His writing explores identity, culture, and the richness of everyday life, often finding the extraordinary in the overlooked.

biography.title: Title
biography.text: John is the founder of Decadent Identities, an interview platform dedicated to celebrating artists, thinkers, and creators. The series is guided by a simple but radical premise: that the people around us, and what we make together, are always worth pausing to honor.

shortStories.title: Title
shortStories.text:
Across the Line

It’s drawn with toes. Etched in as straight a line as the rocks allow.
“That line ain’t right,” Eric says, watching her work backwards in the dirt.
“How would you draw it then?” J’Nae replies.
“Like this,” Eric says, proceeding to add a few lines perpendicular to J’Nae’s long
straight one.
“Hold on, hold on,” J’Nae says, “You tried adding all them lines last time. That’s not the
game I’m thinking of.”
“Well, what game is it?” Eric asks, running the palm of his right foot through the lines
he’d just drawn.
“How ‘bout ‘this side, that side.’ You know it? You know it! Start on that end facing me.
No, not on my side on that side,” she says, arms gesturing where she wants Eric to stand.
“I don’t think I remember this game.” Eric says, his nose scrunched up at having to
switch lanes. “But okay, now what”
“Now you say, ‘this or that?’ If I choose ‘this’ then you answer my question and I take
two steps forward. If I choose ‘that’ then when you answer my question, you’ll be the one taking
two steps forward. You ready?”
“What about my questions?”
“You can ask those once we’ve crossed paths, cool? Cool.”

“I don’t think I like this game,” Eric mumbles.
“Well, we’re playing so it’s too late,” J’Nae says sternly.
Eric plays the disappointment into invisible piano keys with his left hand before asking,
“This or that?”
“This,” she says, a smile flowing on her face, “When was the first time you dug for
gold?”
“Black gold or yellow gold?” he replies, eyes going between J’Nae and the sky.
“The kind you use a finger to get,” she says, with an edge of frustration.
“When I was three. I was sitting outside while Mama trimmed the bushes. The wind was
blowing and I sneezed and scratched. My finger had some gold on it after that so I wiped it in the
grass.”
J’Nae takes two steps forward.
“This or that?” Eric says again.
“That,” J’Nae begins, “What’s something you love more than eating?”
Eric cocks his head to the side, his right hand rising to rub the dimple in his chin. Then,
thinking hard on an answer, his fore and middle fingers graze across pressed lips.
 “Hmm, honestly I love it so much that the only thing that could possibly be better would
be getting eaten,” he finally replies, a serious look on his face as he stares at J’Nae.
“This,” she replies curtly.
“But I haven’t asked the question yet?” Eric says, taking two steps forward.

“Doesn’t matter, I was going to say ‘this’ either way. So who cares if you asked?” she
says and Eric shrugs.
“Oh, now you don’t care if the question is asked or not?” he says grinning.
The two face one another. One on this side of the line, the other on that side. Only two
steps keep them from standing shoulder to shoulder.
“What are thinking about right now?” J’Nae asks.
“What you hope to get out of this?” Eric responds.
“This,” she says, stepping quickly to stand beside him. “What’s your birthday going to be
like this year?”
“Silent and beautiful.”
“That answer’s too vague,” J’Nae says, planting her feet in the dirt.
“Take your two steps,” he replies softly.
J’Nae trudges her feet forward through the dirt.
“I want you to ask,” he says in an almost whisper.
“Thisss ooorrrr that?” she sings in melancholic tone.
“That,” Eric responds, “What was I really like?”
J’Nae swiftly turns to her right. The dust kicked up by her sudden movement blurs her
eyes, crosses the line. Each day it was becoming harder to remember.

shortStories.title: Title
shortStories.text:
He walked the length of the pier without stopping, hands in his pockets, the wind coming off the water sharp and clean. At the end he stood for a long time looking out at the harbor, at the boats rocking in their slips, at the city rising behind him like something remembered rather than seen.

His father used to bring him here on Sundays. They would buy coffee from the cart near the entrance and sit on the bench facing east, watching the ferries come and go. His father never said much on those mornings. Neither did he. It was enough to be there together.

poems.title: Title
poems.text:
After the rain the garden
holds its breath.
Each leaf a small mirror
for what the sky forgot.

I walk the path you laid
before you left—
stone by stone,
the way back in.

poems.title: Title
poems.text:
Winter light on the table.
Your cup still warm.
I read the newspaper
as if you might return.

The clock marks hours
we no longer share.
I fold the page
and set it down.

interviews.title: Title
interviews.text:
Interviewer: When did you first know you wanted to write fiction?

JL: I don't think there was a single moment. It was more like a slow accumulation—a series of small permissions I gave myself. Reading something that moved me and thinking, I want to do that. Not imitate it, but participate in whatever that exchange is between writer and reader.

Interviewer: Your stories often center on ordinary moments. Is that intentional?

JL: Completely. I'm interested in the drama that lives inside what we call uneventful. A conversation that goes slightly wrong. A memory that surfaces at the wrong time. Those are the moments where character reveals itself.

interviews.title: Title
interviews.text:
Interviewer: Who are you reading now?

JL: Too many things at once, as always. I just finished rereading James Baldwin's "Sonny's Blues" for probably the twentieth time. I'm also deep into a novel by a young writer from Lagos whose name I probably shouldn't mention until I've finished.

references.title: Sarah Chen, The New York Times Book Review
references.text: Lewis writes with a precision that makes the ordinary feel luminous. Every sentence earns its place.

references.title: Michael Torres, The Paris Review
references.text: A master of the quiet moment—his stories linger long after the last page.

references.title: Elena Vasquez, n+1
references.text: One of the most distinctive voices in contemporary American fiction.

references.title: David Okonkwo, Granta
references.text: Lewis has the rare ability to find the epic within the everyday.
